entry.title: Bitcoin covenants are inevitable
entry.summary:
The correspondence touches on several critical aspects of Bitcoin development, emphasizing that core development was never intended as a hackathon project. The writer clarifies their independent contribution to the Bitcoin repository, stating a clear distinction between professional acquaintances and personal friends by providing a link to a list of developers who signal their positions on upgrades (find more details [here](https://utxos.org/signals/)). This delineation underscores the professional nature of their interactions within the cryptocurrency development community.
Furthermore, the email addresses the challenges related to funding for Bitcoin developers, highlighting that these financial difficulties affect all developers equally and are not restricted to any specific group. It’s pointed out that competent developers who understand code nuances and soft forks have been active participants in discussions about Consensus-Checked Transaction Verification (CTV). An instance of what the sender perceives as spamming and noise within the community communications is also shared, which can be viewed [here](https://lists.linuxfoundation.org/pipermail/bitcoin-dev/2022-May/020409.html).
Additionally, the sender expresses disinterest in engaging in debates over Bitcoin development strategies or changes proposed by others, including the author of BIP 119. A specific tweet reflecting such topics is referenced, providing insight into public discourse among developers ([view tweet](https://nitter.net/BitcoinErrorLog/status/1407312037408038919)).
Overall, the email encapsulates the complexities and professional dynamics within the Bitcoin development ecosystem, marked by technical discussions, funding issues, and the professional separation between personal affiliations and professional contributions.
